description: Apollo 15 Flight Journal - Day 10, part 4: Orbital Science, Rev 72

tokens: This chapter covers the final period of lunar orbit science before the crew of Apollo 15 begin their preparations to return to Earth.
Date: 4 August 1971.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Endeavour is about to complete rev 71, having just passed out of sight of Earth.
The crew's main task during this far-side pass is to get in some exercise and have their lunch.
Most photography will be by the Mapping and Panoramic cameras in the SIM bay.
Rev 72 will comment in about 20 minutes time and the crew's conversation has been caught on the voice track of the Data Storage Equipment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: FCS refers to the Fecal Containment System.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The DSE onboard recording ceases for about twenty minutes.
Meanwhile, Endeavour's seventy second orbit of the Moon commences at about 217:55 GET.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-330.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Mapping Camera begins operation for the coming daylit pass at 218:17 GET and at about the same time, the Gamma-ray Spectrometer is retracted in preparation for a later test.
The images for this Mapping Camera pass run from AS15-M-2624 to 2752.
At image 2744, the camera is stopped for a while then restarted after the spacecraft has travelled a further 15 degrees in its orbit.
2744 is double exposed.

Beginning with AS15-M-2625, every tenth image in this sequence will be presented here along with 2743, the last good frame before the stoppage, and 2751, the last frame in the sequence that isn't dominated by the landscape beyond the terminator.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As the Mapping Camera is coming on, a sequence of terminator photos is being taken on magazine R of the inner ring of Milne, the southeast rim of Scaliger and the crater Bowditch.
These are AS15-98-13387 to 13393.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The final image in this sequence, AS15-98-13393, is marked in the Apollo 15 photo index as being blank.
With contrast stretching however, detail can be seen and its position has been located for the journal.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Sklodowska is a Polish name.
It is the maiden name of the Polish-French physicist Marie Curie.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Endeavour; this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Hello, Houston; this is Endeavour.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger, Dave.
Looking downstream a little bit here, I know you'll be interested to know that both the shape burn and the satellite jett[ison] will be coming up very close to the nominal times in your Flight Plan.
And we've got the photo PADs here when you're ready to copy, and there are only a few lines.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, Joe.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
The first is a terminator photo PAD to go in at 220 plus 05; and it reads T-start, 220 plus 13 plus 45.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Roger, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Note that there is no record of these photos having been taken in the Apollo 15 Index of 70-mm Photographs.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Thank you, Jim.
And I've got a milestone for you to add here - a Pan Camera photo PAD, with a T-start of 220 plus 15 plus 25.
And the instructions on that are: "Run the Pan Camera to film depletion." And your indication is talkback barber pole.
That's a lot of feet of film.
And finally an entry at 221 plus 59 - it's an addition which reads "circuit breaker O2Tank 100W Heaters, three, Main B, Closed".
Over.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay; on the last one, it was 221:59, "circuit breaker O2Heaters - 100 watt Heaters, three, Main B, Closed".
And then going back to the entry for a - a T-start time of the Pan Camera of 220:15:25, and run the camera to film depletion, and it'll be talkback barber pole.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Right on, Jim.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Endeavour, this is Houston requesting [Mapping Camera] Image Motion to talkback plus 3.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That's in work right now, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Okay; thank you.
And if you give us Accept, we'll update a REFSMMAT to you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: REFSMMATs(reference to a stable member matrix) are used to define a set of orientation vectors to be used by the inertial measurement unit to define, essentially, "which way is up".
In theory, any set of coordinates could be used, but those that represent well defined and easily measured references are best.
Several are rather obvious; the launch pad at the Kennedy Space Center, the landing site of the LM, and the lift-off site of the LM.
What is apparent here is that on a rotating body such as the Moon, Falcon's site on the Moon at lift-off has moved quite a bit since the landing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Al will soon do a double P52 platform realignment and two REFSMMATs are important for this.
The"lift-off" REFSMMAT,which has been used since Falcon's return to orbit, will be used for the first P52 realignment to check on the platform's drift since it was last realigned.
The "TEI" REFSMMAT, which will define the orientation needed for the burn to return to Earth, will be employed for the second P52.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Joe Allen is referring to the TEI REFSMMAT that is being uplinked to the spacecraft about 20 minutes earlier than planned.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Endeavour; Houston again with a couple more items.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Go ahead, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Rog, Jim.

Coming up on our TEC partial Gamma-ray [boom] extension, and looking downstream towards that, we are requesting that you time the Gamma-ray boom extension, which you're going to do at 219 plus 19 - and we'll be standing by for that time.
And also requesting - that you get on the biomed via the Flight Plan, please.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Some time during the Trans-Earth Coast, the Gamma-ray boom will be partially extended.
The way they plan to do this is to time how long a full extension takes.
Then, later, they will be able to give a time for extension which will give a proportionate distance from the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And, Endeavour, it's your computer.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: At 218:52:55, the Panoramic Camera is operated for the penultimate time and will run until they reach the terminator.
Of the 207 images from this pass, all but the last 15 were taken with the camera operating in stereo mode.
This is where the camera nods back and forth between frames so that a forward-looking image will be matched by a rearward-looking image five frames later.
A selection from these images is presented here at the approximate time of taking.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-331.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 21X:XX:XX Allen:This is Houston.
Requesting High Gain, Auto, please.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 218 hours, 59 minutes.
Apollo 15 has 33 minutes remaining in acquisition during the 72nd lunar revolution.
It's been a very quiet pass.
We've accumulated 4 minutes, 27 seconds in tape during the news conference.
We'll play that tape for you now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Endeavour, please terminate battery Bravo charge.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Roger.
Battery Bravo terminating.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Except we were charging battery Alpha.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Joe Allen was mistaken.
It was battery A that was charging.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We copy that, Dave, and agree with it.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Roger.
It's terminated.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And you have - you've got a gotcha on the gentleman sitting next to me.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Ought to hit one once in a while.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 219 hours, 4 minutes.
We're back live on air/ground now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: At around 219:07, the Mapping Camera is stopped.
Joe Allen will ask for it to be restarted after about 5 minutes.
One image, AS15-M-2744, is double exposed due to this stoppage.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Endeavour, Houston.
Turn the Map Camera back On, please.
[Pause.] And it's not necessary to extend it.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Rog [garble].
[Originally transcrobed as 'Map's On'.]
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As a result of the Mapping Camera not being extended on its track, part of its cover is visible on the right of the subsequent pictures
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The next selected image from the current panoramic sequence shows a change in exposure part way through the process of taking the image.
This would have been due to the size of the shutter slit varying as the exposure was being made.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: As Endeavour approaches the sunrise terminator, four photographs are taken on the high-speed magazine R of unremarkable areas of Oceanus Procellarum.
The extremely low-angle lighting makes these normally smooth-looking surfaces appear very rough.
The first two of these are marked in the Apollo 15 photo index as being unidentified, but their positions have been located for the journal.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Endeavour.
This is Houston.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Rog, troops.
We'd like to tag up with you on the recommended TEI procedures.
And, Al, I guess maybe you'll be doing this.
And we - we're interested in reverifying the entries which you've made in your P40 SPS Thrusting Checklist, pages G5-1 through to the end.
And we've got, I guess, two new entries to put in, but we want to reverify the entries that you've already got in there.
Your choice, if you want to read those entries to us, or if you'd like to have us to read them to you.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Mission Control want to verify the items on the cue cards in light of theshort circuit discovered in the SPSon the second day of the mission.

Dealing with the fault, which is in the primary control circuit, requires that they use the secondary, or "B" circuit to start and stop the engine, bringing the A circuit in manually in the middle of the burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
Let's get Al up on the headsets.
Stand by one, please.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Endeavour, Houston.
Requesting Pan Camera Power, Off, please.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Rog.
We're doing it right now.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Okay, Houston.
We had a continuous barber pole on the Pan Camera there.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Roger.
Copy that.
That's a lot of film across those rollers.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Rog.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Endeavour, Houston.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Roger.
A question on your Pan Camera.
When you turned the power off, was it already barber pole or was it gray and then went to barber pole as you threw the switch?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: It was already barber pole.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Okay, thank you.
And we're standing by for Al on the headset.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay; let's - let us get all this SIM bay stuff cleaned up first.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Rog.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Having reached the terminator, they are busy getting the cameras in the SIM bay configured off and someone is timing the deployment of the Gamma-ray boom.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Right now, Al is busy in the Lower Equipment Bay performing two realignments of the guidance platform using the stars Acamar (in the constellation Eridanus - the River) and Fomalhaut (in the constellation Piscis Austrinus - the Southern Fish).
The first reorientation will be to the landing site REFSMMAT.
Once a good alignment is achieved, Mission Control will know by how much the platform has drifted since the previous realignment.
Then the platform will be realigned to the TEI orientation, using the REFSMMAT sent to the spacecraft earlier and the same stars, Acamar and Fomalhaut.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Endeavour; this is Houston.
You're coming up on 8 minutes to LOS.
And we'll just go over the TEI procedures on the next rev.
No hurry on that at all.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, that sounds like a good idea.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And, Houston.
Gamma-ray Boom Deploy was 2 plus 33.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Say again that time, please?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: 2 minutes and 33 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Copy.
Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And, Endeavour; this is Houston with instructions on the Pan Camera.
You can start the Pan Camera power using the normal procedures at T-start.
And if you get an incorrect indication, namely continuous barber pole, just leave it in the normal configuration until AOS.
Over.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Roger.
Understand.
Start it normally at T-start, and if it is in barber pole, just leave it till AOS.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: That's right; thank you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we have the torquing angles.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Worden

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Prior to Al carrying out the P52 realignments, the spacecraft's attitude control is taken away from P20 so it is no longer in its orb-rate motion.
With the realignments completed, control is given back to P20.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Endeavour, you're looking good at LOS minus 60 seconds.
Configure the DSE for us, please.
And we see you've done that.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Roger.
Just did it.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 219 hours, 32 minutes.
We've had Loss Of Signal on the 72nd revolution.
A briefing on orbital science is scheduled for 1 pm today in the MSC Auditorium.
This briefing will not be carried on the Public Affairs release line.
To repeat, the 1 pm briefing on orbital science will not be carried on the Public Affairs release line.
At 219 hours, 32 minutes; this is Mission Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO
